entries.title: Australian GP
entries.headline: Russell Opens 2026 Season With Commanding Melbourne Victory
entries.summary: George Russell delivered a masterclass at Albert Park to claim victory at the 2026 Australian Grand Prix, with Mercedes sending an early statement to the rest of the field.
entries.body:
George Russell led from lights-to-flag in Melbourne to give Mercedes a dominant season opener, controlling the pace throughout and never looking troubled on his way to the chequered flag. A clean start off the front row put him immediately in clear air, and from there the Briton simply managed the gap with the composure that has defined his rise to the top of the sport. Behind him, the battle for the remaining podium positions was fiercely contested, with Lewis Hamilton bringing his Ferrari home in second after a late-race charge pushed him past a fading rival in the closing laps.
The midfield told a more chaotic story, with the new-for-2026 technical regulations producing close and unpredictable racing throughout the pack. Tyre degradation played a bigger role than many teams had anticipated at pre-season testing, catching several strategies out and shuffling the order in the final stint. Lando Norris salvaged a solid points finish for McLaren after a difficult qualifying, while both Audi entries showed genuine race pace that will have rival engineers taking notes. As the paddock heads to Shanghai, Russell leads the drivers' championship and Mercedes sit atop the constructors' standings — exactly the kind of start the Silver Arrows needed after a subdued 2025.

entries.title: Chinese GP
entries.headline: Antonelli Makes History in Shanghai as Mercedes Dominate China Weekend
entries.summary: A weekend to remember in Shanghai — Russell took the sprint, then 19-year-old Kimi Antonelli claimed his maiden Grand Prix win on Sunday to become F1's second youngest ever race winner, with both McLarens failing to even start the race.
entries.body:
Saturday's sprint was a proper scrap. Russell started from pole and immediately had Hamilton breathing down his neck — the Ferrari driver burst forward off the line and actually led in the opening laps before Russell fought back to retake the lead. Behind them Leclerc climbed through to second while Antonelli had a nightmare start, dropped back, got tangled up with Hadjar, and then served a 10-second penalty during the safety car. He still recovered to fifth, which tells you everything about his raw pace. Russell held on to win from Leclerc and Hamilton, keeping his 100% record intact heading into Sunday.

The Grand Prix was where things got really interesting. Antonelli had taken pole in qualifying after Russell hit a gearbox issue, and while Hamilton again got a flying start to briefly lead, Antonelli was back out front before the end of lap two and never gave it up again. Both McLarens — Norris and Piastri — failed to even make the start with electrical issues, handing a massive gift to their championship rivals. Russell recovered from a mid-race scrap with the Ferraris to take second, with Hamilton sealing his first ever podium in Ferrari red in third. At 19 years and 202 days old, Antonelli became just the second teenager in history to win a Grand Prix — only Verstappen was younger. "I'm speechless. I want to cry, to be honest," he said afterwards. Russell meanwhile had his championship lead cut to just four points, and admitted it was going to be a tight year.

entries.title: Japanese GP
entries.headline: Antonelli Does It Again — History Made at Suzuka as Teen Takes Championship Lead
entries.summary: Kimi Antonelli recovered from a terrible start to win his second race in a row at Suzuka, becoming the youngest championship leader in F1 history. Piastri finally finished a race in second, while Russell had a weekend to forget.
entries.body:
Japan was messy from the start — literally. Antonelli bogged down off the line from pole and dropped all the way to sixth in the first few corners, with Russell also losing ground. Piastri pounced brilliantly to lead, and for a while it looked like McLaren might finally get their first win of the season. Piastri and Russell traded positions at the front while Antonelli quietly worked his way back through the field, and when Ollie Bearman crashed heavily on lap 22 to bring out the safety car, the timing couldn't have been better for Mercedes. Antonelli was the only front-runner yet to pit, so he came in under the safety car and emerged in the lead — much to Piastri's frustration, who'd done everything right up to that point.

From the restart Antonelli was untouchable, pulling away to win by 13 seconds and take the championship lead for the first time in his career. At 19 years and 216 days old, he became the first teenager in history to win back-to-back races and the youngest driver ever to lead the drivers' standings — overtaking Ascari as the first Italian to do so since 1953. Piastri took a well-deserved second in what was actually his first race finish of the season after crashing out in Australia and a DNF in China, with Leclerc rounding out the podium. Russell crossed the line fourth and was noticeably unhappy on the radio, with Mercedes acknowledging their rivals are catching up fast heading into the Miami break.

entries.title: Miami GP
entries.headline: Antonelli Makes It Three in a Row as Miami Delivers Carnage, Controversy and a Championship Statement
entries.summary: Kimi Antonelli survived a chaotic Miami weekend — a track limits penalty in the sprint and a messy race start — to win his third consecutive Grand Prix and extend his championship lead to 20 points over Russell.
entries.body:
Saturday's sprint was the first sign all weekend that Mercedes weren't going to have it easy in Miami. All the big teams arrived with upgrades and it showed — Norris took sprint pole, the first non-Mercedes pole of the season, and then led wire to wire in a dominant McLaren one-two with Piastri. Antonelli had another nightmare start, dropped back, got into a scrap with Russell, and then made matters worse by repeatedly exceeding track limits. He crossed the line fourth but was handed a five-second post-race penalty and dropped to sixth, with his championship lead trimmed to just seven points going into Sunday.

The Grand Prix was pure chaos from the opening corners. Antonelli locked up at Turn 1 trying to avoid Leclerc, Verstappen spun out of contention almost immediately, and Hadjar and Gasly both crashed to bring out the safety car early — all within a handful of laps. From there the lead changed hands multiple times between Antonelli, Leclerc and Norris, with the undercut eventually proving the key move as Antonelli pitted at just the right moment to jump Leclerc and get himself out front. From there he held Norris off over the closing laps to win by three seconds and make it three in a row — the first driver to do that since Verstappen's dominant run in 2023. Piastri completed the podium, Russell charged through late to fourth with two big overtakes, and Leclerc spun on the final lap to fall back to sixth. With Russell fourth and Antonelli winning, the championship lead is now 20 points — not a crisis, but the gap between the two Mercedes is clearly tightening.

entries.title: Canadian GP
entries.headline: Russell's Heartbreak Hands Antonelli a Fourth Win as Mercedes' Battle Boils Over in Montreal
entries.summary: The 2026 Canadian Grand Prix had everything — a fiery sprint clash between teammates, a dramatic retirement that decided the race, and McLaren completely falling apart on the wrong tyres. Antonelli now leads by 43 points.
entries.body:
Saturday's sprint set the tone for what was going to be a spicy weekend between the two Mercedes drivers. Russell took pole and held the lead, but Antonelli came for him hard — the pair made contact as Antonelli tried to go around the outside at Turn 1, forcing the Italian off track. Antonelli was left furious on team radio while Russell held on to win from Norris, with the championship leader forced to settle for third. The fact both men showed up at the post-race interview being outwardly respectful didn't hide the fact that the gloves were starting to come off inside the garage.

Sunday was even more dramatic. Russell nicked pole from Antonelli by less than a tenth in qualifying and the pair went at it again in the opening laps of the race, trading the lead back and forth in a genuinely brilliant battle in mixed conditions — McLaren had badly misread the tyre situation at the start, putting both cars on intermediates on a track that was clearly already drying, and it cost them dearly. Then on lap 30, catastrophe for Russell — a power unit failure stopped him dead on track while he was leading and effectively ended his shot at the win. A virtual safety car brought everyone in and Antonelli emerged out front and never looked back, with Hamilton muscling his way past Verstappen late on to take second in a proper battle between two former world champions. For Norris it went from bad to worse, retiring with a gearbox issue after already losing time with a trip across the grass. Antonelli's championship lead is now 43 points — and with Russell getting zero, that gap is starting to feel very real.

entries.title: Monaco GP
entries.headline: Antonelli untouchable in Monaco as penalties wreck Russell's race
entries.summary: Kimi Antonelli made it five wins in a row at Monaco while teammate George Russell scored zero after a pitlane penalty disaster dropped him 68 points behind in the championship.
entries.body:
Verstappen's race ended before it began — the Red Bull lost power on the formation lap and he couldn't even get off the grid, all 21 cars streaming past him before he rolled into retirement. Antonelli was left alone out front from pole and never looked back, building a gap over Hamilton and Leclerc behind while chaos unfolded everywhere else. Norris retired with power unit issues, Bottas overheated and stopped on lap 18, and then the real mess started — five drivers got handed five-second penalties for speeding in the pitlane, likely from cutting the line once they were in it. Hamilton copped one, Russell got one too and then made it worse by failing to serve it at his next stop, which earned him a drive-through penalty, and Gasly somehow managed to pick up two separate speeding penalties. The track surface broke up at the final corner with 10 laps to go, first taking out Stroll and then home hero Leclerc at the restart, triggering a red flag. Gasly crossed the line thinking he'd finished third, only to find out the 10 seconds of penalties had dropped him to seventh — he was near-disconsolate afterwards, calling it heartbreaking.

Antonelli was forced into a second restart after the red flag but stood firm against Hamilton, crossing the line 6.2 seconds clear to take his fifth consecutive win. In doing so he matched Hamilton's run of five straight wins with Mercedes, and at 19 years old became the youngest ever Monaco Grand Prix winner. Hadjar took third for his first Red Bull podium after all the penalties were sorted, with Piastri fourth as the only McLaren left running and both Racing Bulls in the points. Russell finished 12th after serving his drive-through and scored zero points, dropping him behind Hamilton in the standings. Antonelli's championship lead over Russell is now 68 points — and with his teammate having back-to-back DNFs while he's on a five-race winning streak, this title fight is starting to look like a procession.

entries.title: Barcelona-Cataluyna GP
entries.headline: Hamilton Ends Mercedes' Streak With Stunning Barcelona Victory
entries.summary: Lewis Hamilton delivered Ferrari's first win of 2026 in Barcelona, capitalising on a three-stop strategy and a timely VSC to beat George Russell and Lando Norris while championship leader Kimi Antonelli retired late on.
entries.body:
Hamilton had the pace all race — Ferrari's three-stop call had him pulling 1.3 seconds a lap faster than Russell in the middle stint, but the VSC triggered by Alonso's Aston Martin dying on lap 40 was what sealed it. He got a free stop, came out ahead of the Mercedes pair, and that was that. His 106th career win and first ever in red.

The championship story is the big one though. Antonelli had just cleared Russell for second with three laps to go when his power unit gave up — and Leclerc retired moments later with power steering failure. Ferrari took maximum points while Mercedes fell apart at exactly the wrong time. Antonelli's lead in the standings took a significant hit, and with Russell P2 and Norris P3, it's the first all-British podium since 1968. Mercedes will be asking serious questions about why they let Russell and Antonelli race each other the way they did — Toto Wolff called it a "useless race" afterwards. Austria can't come soon enough for Hamilton.

entries.title: Austrian GP
entries.headline: Russell Reigns in Red Bull Ring Chaos
entries.summary: George Russell held off a charging Max Verstappen and Kimi Antonelli to win a fiery Austrian GP, cutting Antonelli's championship lead.
entries.body:
The Austrian GP was a scorcher — both literally and figuratively. Saturday qualifying was proper drama, with George Russell bagging pole under a yellow flag after Max Verstappen binned it at Turn 9. Ferrari boss Fred Vasseur called it "a bit strange", but the FIA cleared Russell, saying he'd slowed enough. Kimi Antonelli, though, was fuming at himself after mistakenly thinking it was a double yellow and aborting his final flying lap, costing him a potential front-row start. "No, I should not have done that. That was my mistake," he admitted. Race day kicked off with absolute chaos, as three cars went up in flames in the opening five laps thanks to the blistering 34-degree heat — Valtteri Bottas and Sergio Perez both retired their Cadillacs with fires, while Liam Lawson somehow kept his Racing Bulls going despite reporting "fire in my car".

Out front, Russell managed to hold his nerve, despite having to push "every single lap" to fend off a persistent Max Verstappen. It was a hard-fought win for Russell, his first since the season opener, and he celebrated by saying, "It's incredible to be back on the top step". Verstappen, who clawed his way to P2 despite some "issues", still found positives in being "that close to a win". Antonelli rounded out the podium in third, just a whisker behind Max, but his championship lead over Russell — once 50 points — has now shrunk to 40. Ferrari, on the other hand, had a tough Sunday — their questionable three-stop strategy for Lewis Hamilton saw him drop to fifth, while Charles Leclerc could only manage P8 after being passed by Lando Norris.

entries.title: British GP
entries.headline: Leclerc Capitalises on Antonelli Heartbreak at Dramatic British GP
entries.summary: Charles Leclerc bagged his first win in over a year at the British GP after championship leader Kimi Antonelli suffered a late mechanical failure,, with the race finishing under Safety Car conditions.
entries.body:
Saturday's sprint was a lively one, with Kimi Antonelli snatching his first Sprint win after a good scrap with polesitter Lewis Hamilton, who initially squeezed him off the line. Antonelli's Mercedes teammate George Russell finished fourth, just ahead of Charles Leclerc. The main race kicked off with immediate drama—Leclerc and Hamilton both jumped Antonelli at the start. Things got spicy quickly, with Ocon, Lawson, and Albon all going off early, and Oscar Piastri reporting damage. To add to the chaos, Hamilton later copped a five-second penalty for a false start.

Antonelli soon took the lead and looked set to extend his championship advantage, despite drivers—like Lewis Hamilton—commenting pre-race on "how poor the power is going to be through this track" with units running out of battery. But Silverstone had other plans. Late in the race, disaster struck for Kimi, who suffered a mechanical failure — a total gut punch for his title hopes. He limped home in ninth, only to then get relegated to sixteenth by a track limits penalty. This opened the door for Leclerc, who hadn't won in over a year, to bag a crucial victory. George Russell picked up P2, and Hamilton salvaged P3 despite his earlier penalty. Even Max Verstappen, who was heard grumbling about "my downshifts are s***" earlier in the race, went off near the end, bringing out the Safety Car and cementing Leclerc's win.

entries.title: Belgian GP
entries.headline: Antonelli Conquers Spa After Russell's Early Exit Drama
entries.summary: Kimi Antonelli bagged his sixth win of the season at Spa, but it wasn't easy — Charles Leclerc pushed him hard. George Russell's title hopes took a huge hit with a Lap 1 DNF after contact with Lewis Hamilton.
entries.body:
Chaos erupted on Lap 1 at Spa-Francorchamps as George Russell's Belgian GP — and potentially his championship hopes — ended abruptly. Going side-by-side with Lewis Hamilton at Les Combes, Hamilton clipped Russell's rear wheel, sending the Mercedes spinning into the gravel. Hamilton copped a five-second penalty for the collision, admitting he "lacked front-end grip", while a visibly emotional Russell told Sky Sports he had "no words at the moment" but pointed to his car's lack of straight-line speed as the root cause, saying, "The incident shouldn't have happened if I had the speed down the straight". The resulting Safety Car and a perfectly timed Virtual Safety Car then played into Ferrari's hands, allowing both Charles Leclerc and Hamilton to take 'cheap' pit stops, propelling Leclerc into the lead. Fred Vasseur, Ferrari Team Principal, was surprised by their strong showing at a track not suited to their car, admitting they were "not expecting this kind of result".

Despite the strategic setback, Antonelli, who started on pole, reeled in Leclerc and made the decisive overtake in the closing stages to clinch his sixth victory of the season. He called it a "hard-fought race" and acknowledged that "Charles was quick, and we had to hold on to that". Max Verstappen, who charged past Antonelli at Eau Rouge on the opening lap, ultimately secured a solid third place, while Hamilton recovered to fourth but now faces a post-race investigation for an unsafe release where he knocked a mechanic during his pit stop. Isack Hadjar and Lando Norris both put in impressive drives to P6 and P7 respectively, recovering well from grid penalties, while Leclerc reflected on his race, noting he was "struggling with the front axle" on the hard tyres before finding more comfort later.
